raw_consumer_statement:
I am disputing a {$1000.00} charge from XXXX, applied to my American Express card on XX/XX/year>, in connection with a ticket sale that I made in good faith but was unable to complete due to third-party platform restrictions and improper guidance from XXXX support . 

I sold tickets on XXXX the day before a basketball game and attempted to transfer them through the official ticketing platform ( XXXX ). However, XXXX blocked the transfer, which I confirmed in writing with their support team. 

I immediately contacted XXXX support, who advised me to bypass their standard process and contact the buyer directly - a workaround that violates XXXX own seller policies. They emailed me the buyers name and phone number and instructed me to meet at the venue. 

I contacted the buyer as instructed. The day of the event, the buyer declined to meet and told me they were buying tickets elsewhere. I did not cancel the sale at any time, and I followed XXXX instructions exactly. I did everything in my power to fulfill the sale under challenging circumstances caused by the merchant itself. Nonetheless, XXXX marked the sale as dropped and charged me a {$1000.00} penalty. 

I submitted evidence to American Express showing : ( 1 ) XXXX 's confirmation that transfers were blocked ; ( 2 ) XXXX  email giving me the buyers contact info; ( 3 ) My call logs showing I contacted XXXX ; and ( 4 ) My texts with the buyer arranging a drop off Despite this, American Express refuses to reverse the charge, stating that I agreed to XXXX  terms and that the merchant has a right to penalize me. However, this ignores the fact that I did not cancel the sale, and that the merchant caused the failure through improper support instructions and platform limitations. This charge was not authorized in spirit or substance, and it is a misuse of stored payment credentials to apply a penalty in a situation that I did not cause.
